model_narrative: We are making one pound of a metal blend by buying and mixing several candidate alloys. Each alloy has a known composition telling how much of each element it contributes, and a known price per pound. We decide how much of each alloy to buy. The objective is to make the total purchase cost as small as possible.

sets.name: alloy
sets.doc: the candidate alloys that can be bought and mixed into the blend

sets.name: elem
sets.doc: the chemical elements whose content in the final blend is controlled

params.name: compdat
params.doc: for each alloy, the percentage of each element it contains and, under the extra label 'price', its purchase price in dollars per pound. The element rows give the percent by weight of that element in the alloy

params.name: rb
params.doc: the required content of each element in the final blend, expressed on the same percent-by-weight basis as the alloy compositions

vars.name: v
vars.doc: the amount of each alloy purchased, in pounds, as a fraction of one pound of finished blend

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: total cost of the alloys bought, in dollars

constraints.name: compdat[elem,
constraints.intent: For each element, the blend has to hit its required content. Add up the contribution of that element from every alloy, weighting each alloy by how much of it is bought, and that total must equal the amount of the element the blend is required to have.

constraints.name: mb
constraints.intent: The amounts of all the alloys bought must together make exactly one pound of finished blend, so the total quantity purchased across every alloy adds up to one.

constraints.name: ac
constraints.intent: The total cost is the price of each alloy multiplied by how much of it is bought, summed over all the alloys. Set the total cost equal to that amount.

query: The CFO is uncomfortable with how concentrated the buy is on alloy a — it's almost half the blend. There's a proposal to cap alloy a at 35% of total purchase by weight going forward. What does the optimizer return for the blend cost under that cap?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_a = Constraint(expr=model.v['a'] <= 0.35 * sum(model.v[a] for a in model.alloy))

Each alloy has a known composition telling how much of each element it contributes, and a known price per pound. We decide how much of each alloy to buy. The objective is to make the total purchase cost as small as possible.

sets.name: elem
sets.doc: the chemical elements whose content in the final blend is controlled

params.doc: for each alloy, the percentage of each element it contains and, under the extra label 'price', its purchase price in dollars per pound. The element rows give the percent by weight of that element in the alloy

params.name: rb

vars.name: v

constraints.name: compdat[elem,
constraints.intent: For each element, the blend has to hit its required content. Add up the contribution of that element from every alloy, weighting each alloy by how much of it is bought, and that total must equal the amount of the element the blend is required to have.

constraints.name: mb
constraints.intent: The amounts of all the alloys bought must together make exactly one pound of finished blend, so the total quantity purchased across every alloy adds up to one.

constraints.name: ac
constraints.intent: The total cost is the price of each alloy multiplied by how much of it is bought, summed over all the alloys. Set the total cost equal to that amount.

query: Procurement wants to keep at least a token presence of the premium alloys e, f, g, h and i on the books so those supplier relationships stay warm. We're evaluating a floor that holds the five of them combined to at least 5% of total purchase by weight. What blend cost does that produce?
expected_pyomo: model.premium_floor = Constraint(expr=model.v['e'] + model.v['f'] + model.v['g'] + model.v['h'] + model.v['i'] >= 0.05 * sum(model.v[a] for a in model.alloy))

query: There's talk of limiting our exposure to alloy d since it's a single-source supplier. Suppose we hold alloy d to no more than 20% of total purchase by weight — where does the blend cost land?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_d = Constraint(expr=model.v['d'] <= 0.20 * sum(model.v[a] for a in model.alloy))

query: The current plan leans hard on a few alloys. Wouldn't it be safer if no single alloy made up more than 30% of total purchase by weight? What's keeping the model from spreading the buy out?

query: Alloys a and b are our two cheapest options. Couldn't we require those two combined to make up at least half the blend by weight? Leaning on the cheap end should beat the current plan, no?
expected_pyomo: model.cheap_floor = Constraint(expr=model.v['a'] + model.v['b'] >= 0.5 * sum(model.v[a] for a in model.alloy))

query: Alloy b never shows up in our blend at all, which seems like a waste of a qualified supplier. Shouldn't we be pulling at least 10% of the buy from b? What's the model seeing that keeps it at zero?
expected_pyomo: model.use_b = Constraint(expr=model.v['b'] >= 0.10 * sum(model.v[a] for a in model.alloy))

query: Right now most of our alloys sit completely unused while the buy piles onto three of them. Couldn't we keep every alloy in the mix at a small minimum — say at least 4% of the buy each — so we're not over-reliant on so few? What's the model weighing against that?
